raw_response:
**Overall rating: MOSTLY_FALSE.** The claim gets the core GitHub behavior wrong: GitHub’s command-line push block is based on whether the commit author email is a **private email on your GitHub account**, not simply whether the email is verified or public.[1][2]

## Short summary
GitHub’s docs say the `Block command line pushes that expose my email` setting blocks pushes when the most recent commit’s author email is a **private email on your account**. GitHub also says that if email privacy is enabled, commits use a no-reply address by default, and if privacy is not enabled you can choose among verified emails for web-based operations. The provided content’s statement that a verified public email will never trigger GH007 is too broad and not supported by the docs.[2][3][1]

## Claim-by-claim analysis

| Claim | Rating | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| “If a GitHub user has a verified email with visibility=public on their account, pushing commits authored with that email will not trigger the GH007 ‘private email’ rejection…” | **MISLEADING** | GitHub says the push block checks whether the commit author email is a **private email on your GitHub account**; it does not say that any verified public email is always exempt in every case [1]. GitHub also distinguishes verified email addresses from privacy settings, and says commit author email choices depend on whether email privacy is enabled [3][2]. |
| “…even when ‘Block command line pushes that expose my email’ is enabled.” | **FALSE** | GitHub’s docs explicitly say that when this setting is enabled, GitHub checks the most recent commit and blocks the push **if the author email is a private email on your GitHub account** [1]. So the setting absolutely can block pushes; it is not bypassed merely because the address is verified. |
| “The block only fires for emails that are private/protected on the account.” | **MOSTLY_TRUE** | This matches GitHub’s documentation closely: the push is blocked when the author email is a **private email on your GitHub account** [1]. The wording “protected” is not GitHub’s own term, but the underlying idea is right [1][2]. |

## Sources
- GitHub Docs: [Blocking command line pushes that expose your personal email address](https://docs.github.com/en/account-and-profile/how-tos/email-preferences/blocking-command-line-pushes-that-expose-your-personal-email-address)[1]
- GitHub Docs: [Setting your commit email address](https://docs.github.com/en/account-and-profile/how-tos/email-preferences/setting-your-commit-email-address)[3]
- GitHub Docs: [Email addresses reference](https://docs.github.com/en/account-and-profile/reference/email-addresses-reference)[2]
